company.name: Cigna
company.description: Cigna provides health insurance, pharmacy benefit management, care coordination, and related health services through Cigna Healthcare and Evernorth Health Services.

overview.title: Business mix
paragraphs: The Cigna Group operates two primary platforms: Cigna Healthcare, which sells health benefits and related services, and Evernorth Health Services, which houses pharmacy benefit services, specialty pharmacy, care services, and related health-service infrastructure.

Its market power comes from a combination of employer and government-plan relationships, negotiated provider and pharmacy networks, formulary design, claims processing, specialty pharmacy scale, and care-management workflows.

overview.title: Registry relevance
paragraphs: Cigna is a high-friction incumbent because insurance, PBM, provider contracting, pharmacy routing, prior authorization, and claims data all depend on regulated networks and dense operational relationships.

The strongest free-world pressure is unlikely to be a simple clone of Cigna. More plausible pressure comes from open health records, patient-directed claims data, transparent purchasing networks, cooperative benefits administration, and protocol-based audit trails that reduce dependence on closed intermediaries.

moatNarrative: Cigna's moat is strong because it sits inside multiple regulated coordination layers: employer benefits, provider networks, pharmacy benefit management, specialty pharmacy, utilization management, and claims administration. Each layer benefits from scale, negotiated contracts, compliance operations, actuarial data, and switching friction.

Evernorth strengthens the moat by giving Cigna a large role in drug benefit design, formulary management, pharmacy network administration, and specialty pharmacy workflows. That also creates regulatory and reputational exposure because PBM concentration and vertically integrated incentives are under sustained scrutiny.
decentralizationNarrative: Cigna is only partly decentralizable as a full-service insurer because risk pooling, solvency regulation, employer procurement, provider contracting, and claims adjudication remain institution-heavy. A purely peer-to-peer replacement would struggle to satisfy reserve, compliance, network adequacy, and fraud-control requirements.

Parts of the stack are more contestable. Patient-controlled health records, FHIR-based claims access, open-source clinical systems, transparent drug-pricing cooperatives, and auditable prior-authorization workflows can chip away at the closed data and coordination advantages that make integrated insurers and PBMs hard to displace.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 126-150.

moat.rationale: Cigna combines regulated insurance operations, employer relationships, pharmacy benefit management, specialty pharmacy, claims data, provider and pharmacy networks, and negotiated purchasing scale. These assets create high switching costs and compliance barriers.

decentralizability.rationale: The full insurer and PBM stack is difficult to decentralize because it depends on regulated risk pooling, solvency, contracting, adjudication, and fraud controls. Specific layers such as records access, transparent purchasing, and claims workflows are more open to protocol and cooperative approaches.

profitability.rationale: Cigna remains a large profitable health-services company with durable operating income from its two platforms, although insurance utilization, PBM scrutiny, and pricing regulation can pressure margins.

peRatio.rationale: Market data showed Cigna trading near 12 times earnings on May 27, 2026, which is useful as a point-in-time valuation input rather than a stable operating metric.

marketCap.rationale: Market data listed The Cigna Group at roughly $75.33 billion of market capitalization on May 27, 2026.

products.name: Cigna Healthcare
products.summary: Cigna Healthcare provides medical, dental, behavioral health, and related benefit products and services for employers, individuals, and government-linked markets.
products.whyItMatters: It is the public-facing insurance and benefits layer where members, employers, providers, and brokers interact with Cigna's network, pricing, claims, and care-management systems.
replacementSketch: A realistic replacement path would start with narrower benefit pools, self-funded employer groups, direct primary care networks, open records, transparent claims administration, and member-owned data access instead of trying to recreate a national insurer in one jump.

Open-source clinical records and FHIR-based claims portability can make care coordination less dependent on a single benefits administrator, while cooperative purchasing groups could handle some employer-benefit functions with clearer governance.

alternatives.name: OpenMRS
alternatives.summary: OpenMRS is an open-source electronic medical record platform maintained by a global community.

openness.rationale: OpenMRS is explicitly open source and publishes its community and code through public project infrastructure.

decentralizationFit.rationale: OpenMRS does not replace insurance, but it helps shift clinical data systems away from closed payer or vendor stacks and toward deployable community-run infrastructure.

readiness.rationale: The project has long-running releases, documentation, and real-world deployments, but adoption still requires local implementation, hosting, clinical workflow design, and integration work.

costLeverage.rationale: Open-source records software can reduce licensing lock-in and improve portability, though implementation and support costs remain material in healthcare settings.

alternatives.name: GNU Health
alternatives.summary: GNU Health is a free/libre health and hospital information system for health practitioners, institutions, and governments.

openness.rationale: GNU Health is part of the GNU free software ecosystem and positions itself around software freedom and equity in healthcare.

decentralizationFit.rationale: GNU Health can support local public-health and institutional deployments, but it does not by itself decentralize insurance risk pools or payer contracting.

readiness.rationale: The project is usable and source-available, but production healthcare deployment requires technical capacity, compliance work, and integration with local clinical and billing processes.

costLeverage.rationale: Free software can reduce vendor dependence and licensing costs, especially for public-health or institutional systems, while still requiring implementation and support budgets.

disruptionConcepts.name: Cooperative Benefits Data Union
disruptionConcepts.summary: A member- and employer-owned benefits administrator could combine open health-record tooling, FHIR-based claims access, and cooperative purchasing governance to negotiate care and administer benefits without handing all data and routing power to a vertically integrated insurer.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept attacks Cigna Healthcare's switching-cost and data-control moat by making claims history, care plans, and benefits administration portable across cooperative operators.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through member governance, portable data, and multi-operator administration rather than through Bitcoin itself. The core shift is from one insurer-controlled operating system to a federated benefits network with auditable rules.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Employers, members, clinics, and administrators coordinate through shared plan rules, portable FHIR records, cooperative governance, and transparent service-level agreements for claims and care-management tasks.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Claims events would be reconciled against provider attestations, member-visible explanations of benefits, standardized audit logs, and independent plan audits. Fraud controls still require identity checks, medical necessity review, and regulated administrators.
failureModes: Cooperatives may lack enough covered lives to negotiate competitive provider rates.
Regulatory reserve, licensing, privacy, and fraud-control obligations could recreate centralized administrators.
Provider data quality and interoperability gaps could limit portability.
adoptionPath: Begin with self-funded employers or associations that already control plan design and can contract for third-party administration.
Use FHIR claims access and open-source clinical tooling to build member-visible longitudinal records.
Add cooperative governance, transparent procurement, and independent auditing once the administrator has enough covered lives.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept decentralizes data access and governance but still needs regulated administrators and contracted care networks.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Self-funded employers, FHIR APIs, and open health records provide credible coordination primitives, but insurance administration remains operationally complex.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Feasible in narrow employer or association markets, but difficult to scale nationally because of provider contracting, reserves, compliance, privacy, and fraud controls.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept could pressure administrative fees and member data lock-in, but it would not quickly replace Cigna's network scale or actuarial infrastructure.

products.name: Evernorth
products.category: Pharmacy benefit and health services platform
products.summary: Evernorth is Cigna's health-services platform for pharmacy benefit services, specialty pharmacy, care services, and related affordability and access programs.
products.whyItMatters: Evernorth is central to Cigna's role in drug purchasing, pharmacy networks, formulary design, claims adjudication, specialty dispensing, and care-management economics.
replacementSketch: A plausible replacement would not start as a full PBM clone. It would begin with transparent formularies, pass-through pricing, open audit trails, cooperative purchasing, and patient-visible pharmacy routing for narrower drug categories or employer groups.

Over time, federated purchasing pools and transparent adjudication software could reduce the need for opaque rebate-driven intermediaries, especially where plan sponsors value auditability over maximum black-box rebate extraction.

alternatives.name: CMS Blue Button 2.0
alternatives.summary: CMS Blue Button 2.0 is a standards-based API that lets Medicare beneficiaries authorize applications to access Medicare claims data using FHIR and OAuth 2.0.

openness.rationale: Blue Button 2.0 is a public standards-based API using FHIR and OAuth, though it is a government Medicare data service rather than a fully open replacement for PBM systems.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It improves patient-directed claims portability and allows third-party applications to build around beneficiary-authorized data access.

readiness.rationale: The API is documented and operational for Medicare claims access, but it is limited to Medicare data and does not perform PBM procurement or adjudication by itself.

costLeverage.rationale: Claims portability can reduce information lock-in and improve auditability, but drug purchasing leverage still depends on contracting and plan design.

disruptionConcepts.name: Transparent PBM Clearing Network
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federated PBM clearing network could let employers, pharmacies, wholesalers, and plan members coordinate around transparent acquisition costs, pass-through administration fees, portable formularies, and independently auditable claim events.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept changes the PBM market from a few vertically integrated, opaque intermediaries into a network of auditable administrators competing on execution, pharmacy access, and verified savings.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federation and auditability, not tokenization. Multiple administrators could run compatible adjudication and reporting nodes while plan sponsors retain the ability to compare pricing and move business.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Plan sponsors publish benefit rules and formularies, pharmacies submit claims through compatible clearing nodes, wholesalers and pharmacies expose contractually auditable price references, and members receive portable explanations of benefit and pharmacy-routing options.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: The network would constrain cheating through signed claim events, independent audits, pharmacy and wholesaler attestations, reconciliation against paid claims, and member-visible pricing records. It would still need legal contracts and regulators for rebate, privacy, and anti-kickback compliance.
failureModes: Large PBMs may retain better manufacturer and pharmacy economics because of scale.
Manufacturers and pharmacies may resist exposing price references or rebate-equivalent concessions.
Transparent rules can still be gamed through coding, substitution, steering, or selective network design.
adoptionPath: Start with self-funded employers demanding pass-through pricing and audit rights.
Implement transparent formularies and signed claims logs for selected therapeutic categories.
Federate multiple administrators once plan sponsors can compare outcomes and move covered lives without rebuilding the full stack.

decentralizationFit.rationale: A federated clearing model directly targets centralized PBM routing and opaque reporting, while still depending on regulated contracts and audits.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Plan sponsors already contract for PBM services and can demand audit rights, but manufacturer rebates, pharmacy networks, and specialty drug economics are hard to coordinate transparently.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Technically plausible for claims logs and reporting, but commercially difficult because large PBMs control network scale and rebate relationships.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If regulators and plan sponsors keep pushing transparency, a credible audit-first PBM model could pressure spread pricing, steering, and rebate opacity.
